narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dreams despite the challenges many face in this field, and I want to help you understand the automated decision that led to your application's rejection in a clear way. The admissions model looks at patterns from past students—like their grades, test scores, family background, and study plans—to predict the chances of passing the bar exam on the first try, and unfortunately, it estimated your risk of failing at 43%, which crossed the threshold for denial. This prediction was driven mostly by your Year 3 school decile ranking, which was quite low compared to most applicants' averages, strongly pushing the model toward seeing a higher failure risk. On a brighter note, your solid Year 1 decile, which was above average, worked in your favor by suggesting stronger early potential and pulling the prediction toward success. Your undergraduate GPA, also a bit below the typical level, added some concern but had a milder downward pull, while your upper-middle family income helped nudge things positively, as it often correlates with better resources for success. Being a part-time student was another favorable factor, since fewer applicants choose that path and it sometimes signals better balance. Even your LSAT score, lower than the average, had only a small negative influence overall. Compared to typical students—who often have higher Year 3 rankings, stronger GPAs, and full-time plans—your profile stood out in areas like early grades and background but was held back most by that later academic dip. This isn't a judgment on your worth or potential as a Black woman breaking barriers in law; it's just the model's data-driven snapshot. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT or boosting your recent grades through targeted prep or additional coursework to reshape your profile for future applications. Many students in similar situations have succeeded by addressing these key areas—reach out to advisors for personalized strategies, and know that persistence like yours often leads to breakthroughs. We're rooting for you.
